title: Price elasticity of demand and risk-bearing capacity in sovereign bond auctions
abstract: The paper uses bids submitted by primary dealer banks at auctions of sovereign bonds to quantify the price elasticity of demand. The price elasticity of demand correlates strongly with the volatility of returns of the same bonds traded in the secondary market but only weakly with their bid-ask spread. It predicts same-bond post-auction returns in the secondary market, even after controlling for pre-auction volatility. The evidence suggests that the price elasticity of demand is associated with the magnitude of price pressure in the secondary market around auction days and proxies for primary dealer risk-bearing capacity.

author.José Miguel Cardoso-Costa: Banco de Portugal and Nova School of Business and Economics,Portugal
author.José Afonso Faias: Católica Lisbon School of Business and Economics,Portugal

article.1 Introduction:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Ralph Koijen Ralph Koijen Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar
This paper studies the behavior of primary dealers in the auction market for sovereign bonds. Primary dealers’ fragility was a point of vulnerability of the U.S. financial sector during the 2007-2009 crisis (Duffie 2013).Goldberg (2020)shows that declines in broker-dealer inventory capacity are associated with future drops in liquidity across several asset classes and the availability of financing, andHe, Kelly, and Manela (2017)show that shocks to the equity capital of primary dealers predict the cross-section of returns across a broad class of assets (see alsoEtula 2013). These papers suggest that primary dealers’ risk-bearing capacity can contribute to aggregate risk. We study primary dealers’ bidding in auctions of sovereign bonds and the elasticity of demand thus revealed as a proxy for their risk-bearing capacity.

Several papers have documented a puzzling pattern of secondary market prices in reaction to uninformative supply (and demand) shocks that suggest that the demand for financial assets is not perfectly elastic, even in the most liquid markets (e.g.,Duffie 2010). Possibly the prime example occurs in the U.S. sovereign bond market, which is one of the most liquid in the world (e.g.,Fleming 2003).Fleming, Nguyen, and Rosenberg (2022)andLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)find that, around auction days, the secondary-market yields of bonds of similar maturity to those being auctioned (and sometimes the same bond) display an inverted V-shaped pattern.1The authors argue that this finding is consistent with primary-dealer banks’ limited risk-bearing capacity and thus consistent with an imperfectly elastic demand curve. Still, the lack of more direct evidence on the elasticity of demand as an explanation of the puzzling evidence –and of other similar price-pressure effects– leaves room for alternative hypotheses and may explain why still most asset pricing models assume perfectly elastic demand.2

The elasticity of demand is the marginal increase in quantity demanded by investors for a marginal decrease in the price of the bond. In a perfectly competitive market, an asset’s price elasticity of demand is infinite, meaning that investors absorb any shock to supply at the equilibrium price. In contrast, in models of slow-moving capital, primary dealers’ limited risk-bearing capacity gives rise to a less-than-perfectly elastic demand. Because an auction significantly increases primary dealers’ bond holdings in the short run, their ability to absorb this shock likely depends on several factors: their inventory; how much they can offload of their portfolios prior to the auction in the secondary market (provided they know which bonds are being auctioned);3dealers’ expectation of the ease with which they will be able to later turn over to other investors bonds purchased at the auction (ie, their expectation of others’ risk-bearing capacity); and, possibly, other secondary-market, bond-specific characteristics, such as return volatility and liquidity. Thus, we expect these secondary-market conditions to be reflected in the elasticity of demand in the primary market. Specifically, we hypothesize that, if the elasticity of demand at the auction is high, then primary dealers expect to be able to sell acquired bonds quickly, and we should find no significant price movements around the auction. In contrast, with a low elasticity, we hypothesize both a price drop and a slow price reversal.

Duffie (2010)argues for a link between the price elasticity of demand and risk-bearing capacity in models of slow-moving capital: “Prices respond to supply or demand shocks with a sharp reaction because of the relatively small subset of capital (and thus risk-bearing capacity) that is immediately available to absorb a shock on short notice.”4In addition, according toKyle (1989), the slope of an informed investor’s auction demand function decreases in absolute value with risk aversion and asset volatility. Also,Johnson (2006)shows that, in the Lucas model and with general preferences, the investor has a negatively sloped demand curve that relates to risk aversion. A liberal interpretation of risk aversion for financial agents is that these agents penalize volatility for reasons including inventory costs, portfolio constraints (Allen and Wittwer 2021), and the difficulty in finding demand due to inattentive investors (e.g.,Duffie 2010). We therefore interpret a low elasticity of demand as suggesting low risk-bearing capacity.

We analyze a unique, proprietary bid-level dataset from the Portuguese Treasury and Debt Management Agency (Portuguese acronym IGCP). The data used in the main analysis contain all the bids for 66 bond auctions conducted by the Portuguese Treasury from 2014 to 2019. Auction-bid data are generally unavailable for other markets, including the U.S. There are four institutional features of the Portuguese market that can be used to provide better identification for a test of the risk-bearing capacity hypothesis. First, all auctions are re-openings of bonds already traded in the secondary market (originally sold via syndication), so their effects can be measured in the primary and secondary markets. In contrast, in the U.S. sovereign bond market and apart from the when-issued market typically in place only a few days before the auction, most auctions are not re-openings, which means that the researcher can only track the secondary-market prices of bonds of similar maturities, which are by construction imperfect substitutes. Second, only primary dealers can participate in the auctions, whereas, in the U.S. market, other institutional investors can also do so. Risk-bearing capacity of primary dealers (which may depend indirectly on the risk-bearing capacity of other market participants) can be better identified in the Portuguese market. Third, T-bond auctions occur on pre-determined Wednesdays of each month. Lastly, the lines to be auctioned and an indicative issuance interval are announced on the Friday prior to the Wednesday of the auction. The IGCP keeps the issue size close to the top of the interval, though the exact auction size is only known on the auction day. The last two features imply that T-bond auctions are quasi-uninformative supply shocks anticipated by three trading days.

We find that the Portuguese government secondary T-bond market displays a V-shaped price pattern around T-bond auctions. On average, the secondary-market price of the bond being issued drops by nine basis points in the three trading days prior to an auction and subsequently increases by six basis points, relative to a benchmark representing the performance of all Portuguese government bonds. In the robustness section, we show that raw yields present the same inverted V-shape as inLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)orBeetsma et al. (2016). We conduct a placebo test by examining unused auction dates by the IGCP. We do not observe a V-shaped curve in the secondary-market price at any bond maturity for these placebo dates.

For our main results, we compute a measure of the elasticity of aggregate demand at each auction usingunsubscribedbids next to the cutoff price. This elasticity measures the price drop that the IGCP would have to accept if it were to increase the supply of the bond at the margin. It indicates the remaining liquidity in the auction, which we use as a proxy for dealers’ risk-bearing capacity. There are several noteworthy properties of the absolute value of thismarginal elasticityof demand.5First, the average value of this elasticity is 332. According to this estimate, an increase in quantity supplied at an auction by roughly 3% is accommodated with only a one basis point drop in the price on average. This large elasticity suggests that the Portuguese sovereign bond market has been fairly liquid since 2014. For comparison, the average value we estimate is more than 10 times larger thanKandel, Sarig, and Wohl (1999)’s estimated average elasticity in Israeli IPO auctions.

Second, in the cross-section of all the auctions, the marginal elasticity has a strong negative correlation with the pre-auction volatility of the secondary-market returns of the bond being auctioned. This negative correlation suggests that the two variables may be proxies for dealer risk-bearing capacity, since return volatility is an important metric related to dealer inventory risk (e.g.,Kyle 1989). The marginal elasticity also has a strong negative correlation with the bid-ask spread, but its significance disappears when controlling for the return volatility. This result suggests that the marginal elasticity has additional information content relative to the bid-ask spread, a usual proxy for market liquidity. Further, the marginal elasticity has a positive (though insignificant) correlation with the three-day price drift prior to the auction, which is to be expected if a lower elasticity is associated with increased price pressure.

Third, we find that the documented V-shaped pattern is only statistically significant in the subsample of auctions where the measured elasticity is low (ie, below the sample median). This finding comports with the hypothesis that there is only price impact when primary dealers anticipate that they cannot quickly offload bonds in the secondary market. For the low-elasticity subsample, the price drops by about 11 basis points in the three trading days prior to the auction date, increasing by about 21 basis points in the subsequent five trading days, relative to the benchmark. For comparability, the average bid-ask spread in our sample is 22 basis points.

Fourth, we run in-sample predictive regressions ofpost-auctionsecondary-market bond abnormal returns at various holding horizons on the marginal elasticity. To control for time variation in risk premia, we include in the regressions the (pre-auction) return volatility of the bond being auctioned. Other controls include the relative bid-ask spread, the price drift in the three trading days prior to the auction, and the bid-to-cover ratio (Beetsma et al. 2018). The marginal elasticity has a negative coefficient at every horizon and is statistically significant at all horizons up to 10 days, except for the two-day horizon. The negative coefficient indicates that a low value of the elasticity (suggestive of low risk-bearing capacity) is associated with future price increases as predicted. A decrease in the marginal elasticity by one standard deviation predicts an 11 basis point increase in the price of the bond after five days. Price drift before the auction carries a negative sign for all holding periods, suggesting that larger price declines before the auction are associated with larger price increases afterward, as would be predicted if the prior price movement was motivated by price pressure, but this coefficient is statistically significant only up to the horizon of five days. The coefficient on return volatility has a positive sign as predicted but is statistically insignificant. This suggests that the return volatility, price drift, and the marginal elasticity may all proxy for primary dealers’ risk-bearing capacity, and the marginal elasticity appears to be the least noisy of the three. These results are consistent with dealer-level evidence ofAllen and Wittwer (2021), who show that the steepness of individual dealer demand in Canadian Treasury auctions is positively associated with dealer capitalization. Finally, the relative bid-ask spread has a positive and generally significant coefficient, consistent with capturing a liquidity premium associated with transaction costs.

Fifth, we provide one additional test of the mechanism by studying the relationship for bonds with different maturities. We expect the effect of the marginal elasticity to be more pronounced for longer-duration bonds, as these generally have higher interest rate risk and are thus more prone to impact traders’ profit volatility. In fact, the IGCP recognizes dealers’ lower willingness to trade longer-duration bonds by benefiting dealers that participate more actively in these auctions. When we add a short duration dummy and its interaction with the marginal elasticity to the predictive regression controls, we find an increase in the economic and statistical significance of the marginal elasticity on predictive regressions of excess returns across all holding horizons. This evidence suggests, as expected, that the effect of the marginal elasticity strengthens for longer-duration bonds. The effect on short-duration bonds remains negative but statistically insignificant.

Perhaps the main alternative hypothesis to the primary dealers’ limited risk-bearing capacity and one unrelated to the elasticity of demand is the transaction cost story (Shleifer 1986). Consistent with this hypothesis and taking the bid-ask spread as a measure of transaction costs, our results show that the bid-ask spread is negatively correlated with the price drift in the pre-auction period and positively predicts price movement following the auction. Importantly, the statistical significance ofMEin the predictive regressions is unaffected by the inclusion of the bid-ask spread. The transaction cost hypothesis also predicts underpricing at the auction–as primary dealers will have to resell the auctioned amount of the bond and pay transaction costs and will thus transfer some of these costs back to the Treasury via an underpricing–while our data shows overpricing on average.

Another alternative explanation is the liquidity hypothesis: if the auctioned bond is being neglected prior to the auction, then it will earn a higher return if its salience increases with the auction (seeShleifer 1986). This hypothesis is less relevant in our setting, as the market participants are well informed, large institutional investors. Further, the liquidity hypothesis would not predict a price drop ahead of the auction, which we find, but rather an increase. Third, the literature on price pressure often examines events that are uncommon for firms, such as an index inclusion. These rare events may change the profile of the firm allowing it to tap into new and more abundant capital, which is then associated with higher valuations (seeChen, Noronha, and Singal 2004). This alternative hypothesis is unlikely in our setting, as we are seeing the same issuer over time, sometimes issuing the same bond repeatedly in a short period. Fourth,Duffie (2010)andSigaux (2020)discuss the alternative hypothesis that price pressure is due to an inability to contemporaneously observe the supply shock. In our setting, the auction size is fairly predictable, as it often occurs at the maximum of the indicative interval announced ahead of the auction. Still, in our tests, we include a control for how the auction size deviates from the top of the indicative interval and find no change in results. Finally,Amin and Tédongap (2023)andFleming, Nguyen, and Rosenberg (2022)note that primary dealers absorbed a decreasing fraction of the auction sales of U.S. sovereign debt post 2012 in favor of investment funds.Amin and Tédongap (2023)show that, despite the investor composition change in the TIPS market post 2012, the V-shaped pattern does not disappear. They interpret this evidence as suggesting that it is not primary dealers’ risk-bearing capacity that is causing the pattern in yields. In our data, only primary dealers can acquire bonds sold at auctions, and moreover there is considerable stability over time in the dealers who participate at the auctions. Hence, ours is a clean setting for testing the hypothesis that primary dealers’ risk-bearing capacity determines price pressure. In light of our evidence, we reinterpret the findings inAmin and Tédongap (2023)as suggesting that it is the aggregate risk-bearing capacity of the various market participants present in the auction that determines the amount of price pressure.

In a robustness test, we study the COVID-19 pandemic year of 2020. During that time, the volatility in financial markets and the response of central banks is likely to have affected the risk-bearing capacity of primary dealer banks. We do not include data from 2020 in our main analysis, so as not to taint the results in any direction. Next, motivated by the existence of bid shading (e.g.,Hortaçsu and Kastl 2012andHortaçsu, Kastl, and Zhang 2018), we add the bid discount observed in the auction as a control variable in the predictive regressions. Third, we modify the procedure used to calculate the price elasticity of demand in several ways, including changing the scaling factor to reflect the total outstanding of the bond. These changes do not qualitatively affect our results.

The next section presents the related literature. Section 3 briefly describes the institutional setting of the Portuguese T-bond auctions and provides pertinent statistics. Section 4 discusses estimates of the price elasticity of demand and its determinants and introduces the remaining variables we use. Section 5 presents the main results. Section 6 discusses robustness tests, and section 7 concludes.
article.2 Related literature:
Recent literature points to primary dealer risk-bearing capacity as a key driver of financial asset prices.Adrian, Etula, and Muir (2014)show that shocks to the leverage of securities broker-dealers help explain cross-sectional variation in expected returns of stocks and bonds.Etula (2013)shows that financial assets and liabilities for U.S. security broker-dealers contain information that can explain commodity returns.He, Kelly, and Manela (2017)show that the equity capital ratio of primary dealer counterparties of the New York Federal Reserve can explain the returns on a broad set of assets.

Several papers consider price pressure effects due to financial intermediation constraints.Gabaix and Koijen (2021)reveal significant price pressure in the stock market, which they hypothesize derives from the behavior of constrained financial intermediaries (see alsoHaddad, Huebner, and Loualiche 2021). They use a structural model to infer an elasticity consistent with price changes around capital flows into the stock market.6Hendershott and Menkveld (2014)provide evidence consistent with intermediaries in the New York Stock Exchange causing price pressure to mean-revert their inventory.Bogousslavsky, Collin-Dufresne, and Saǧlam (2021)use the trading induced by an exogenous trading glitch to study the price response of the affected stocks. In a dealers’ market,Hansch, Naik, and Viswanathan (1998)show that an increase (decrease) in inventory may cause the market maker to go from offering a best bid (ask) to offering a best ask (bid).Gromb and Vayanos (2010)survey the literature that discusses the implications of institutional constraints for the ability of arbitrageurs to exploit apparent market mispricing.Duffie (2010)discusses evidence consistent with slow moving capital, andDu, Tepper, and Verdelhan (2018)provide evidence of systematic violations of covered interest parity that are not arbitraged away. The asset pricing literature has identified price pressure effects from shocks to both supply and demand (e.g.,Shleifer 1986,Harris and Gurel 1986,Loderer, Cooney, and Drunen 1991,Kaul, Mehrotra, and Morck 2000,Wurgler and Zhuravskaya 2002,Coval and Stafford 2007,Lou 2012,Wardlaw 2020, andCamanho and Faias 2020).Hartzmark and Solomon (2021)show that aggregate dividend payments to investors, which are uninformed and predictable flows, predict stock market returns around the day of payment.

Our paper extends the literature on the predictability of secondary-market yield variations around Treasury auctions.Fleming, Nguyen, and Rosenberg (2022)andLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)interpret their findings as reflecting hedging by primary dealers with limited risk-bearing capacity that is insufficiently accompanied by other investors.Lou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)document that the V-shaped pattern is more pronounced for larger auction sizes, at times of low growth rate of aggregate broker-dealer leverage, or when the volatility of interest rates is high (see alsoBeetsma et al. 2016). They also discuss how the phenomenon differs from the on-the-run premium. In subsequent work,Beetsma et al. (2018)show that euro-area sovereign auctions with high bid-to-cover ratios see a more pronounced increase in the secondary-market price on the day of the auction.Forest (2018)observes a similar finding in the U.S. 30-year T-bond market.
article.3 Institutional background and auction d:
ThePortuguese Treasury and Debt Management Agency(IGCP) has conducted T-bond auctions using a uniform-price system since April 2014.7Data on auctions prior to 2011 (and on T-bill auctions) are available, but these auctions used a multi-price method. For the purpose of this study, using data on uniform-price auctions allows for comparability with results in U.S. Treasury auctions (e.g.,Lou, Yan, and Zhang 2013), where uniform-price auctions have been used exclusively since the late 1990s. Canadian auctions of sovereign debt, widely used in other studies (e.g.,Hortaçsu and Kastl 2012), follow a multi-price method, where estimating an aggregate demand function is challenging (Chang 2023).

The IGCP auctions T-bonds using a primary dealership model. A small group of financial intermediaries (the primary dealers) participate and are responsible for marketing Portuguese debt securities to final investors and ensuring liquidity in the secondary market. The auctions comprise a competitive phase, where each participant may submit multiple bids, each comprising a bidding quantity in multiples of €1 million and a bidding price in multiples of €0.01, without exceeding the upper limit of the auction indicative amount. There is no minimum bid in each auction, but each primary dealer is expected to take up at least 2% of the total amount issued through auctions every two years. In return, the IGCP grants dealers intermediation gains resulting from exclusive direct access to the primary market, access to a post-auction noncompetitive bidding phase, and issuance fees when selected as lead managers in syndicated deals.

During our sample period, T-bond auctions can occur on pre-determined dates, the second, fourth, or fifth Wednesdays of each month (though in the whole sample only two auctions occur in the month of August and none in the month of December). The week prior to each of these days, the IGCP contacts primary dealers to solicit their views on market conditions: it collects their opinions on whether an auction should be conducted, the lines to be auctioned, and the issuance amounts. On the afternoon of the Friday prior to the auction, the IGCP announces whether an auction will occur, and, if so, it discloses the specific security (or securities) to be issued and the indicative issuance amount (typically announced as an interval for the total to be raised across the lines being offered). In practice, the IGCP has tended to use only one of the available windows in each month, so the likelihood of conducting an auction when the previous window had not been used was relatively high.

Two institutional characteristics allow us to treat these auctions as quasi-uninformative supply shocks. First, T-bond auctions occur on pre-determined Wednesdays, following a predictable funding strategy: the IGCP announces a total annual issuance amount in the beginning of each year that is typically distributed evenly over the year. Second, dealers learn about the auctioned lines on the Friday prior to each auction, and, at the announcement, the IGCP also announces an indicative issuance interval for the total of the lines. In practice, the IGCP issues at or close to the maximum of the indicative range in most auctions so the market can anticipate the total size of the issue. Additionally, all auctions are re-openings of existing bonds, which are already traded in the secondary market. This allows us to study the secondary-market price effects of the auction. Lastly, an institutional characteristic of the Portuguese bond auctions that helps us identify the risk-bearing capacity of primary dealers is that only registered primary dealers can participate in Portuguese auctions. With exception of the last feature, the US market shares all the same characteristics (e.g., seeLou, Yan, and Zhang 2013). Canadian sovereign debt auctions are also pre-announced (lines are announced at the beginning of each quarter and the issue size announced one week prior to the auction), and, as in the U.S., indirect bidding by non-dealers is allowed besides direct bidding by dealers.

We obtain unique, proprietary bid-level data from the IGCP. The data include all bids submitted by each primary dealer in all 92 T-bond auctions conducted between 2014 and 2020. The main analysis focuses on data through the end of 2019, that is, 74 auctions. We exclude from our main analysis five auctions with an issuance amount equal to the minimum of the indicative amount and three other auctions whose issue size is within a small margin of 5% of the minimum indicative amount, resulting in a final sample of 66 auctions. We filter these auctions because we want to focus on auctions whose size did not surprise investors and hence were more likely uninformative.8Later, we also study the effects of the 2020 COVID-19 crisis, but that analysis excludes the two auctions in March 2020, resulting in a total of 82 observations.

Table 1reports summary statistics of T-bond auctions per year. In the beginning of the sample, which coincides with the aftermath of the sovereign debt crisis, the number of auctions is low, with only four auctions conducted in 2014. Portugal was slowly returning to issuing T-bonds, and syndicated deals played a more important role. Since 2016, the number of T-bond auctions has oscillated between 13 and 16. T-bonds are issued with maturities between two and 30 years with an average duration between seven and 11 years.SIZEdescribes the total subscribed amount. The auctions in 2014 and 2015 have an average size close to €1 billion; auction size declines to about €600 million from 2016 onward, a consequence of the IGCP starting to conduct regular simultaneous auctions on two lines.

Over the sample period, there are 24 registered primary dealers, of which 19 are present in all years. In any single year, the mode of the number of registered dealers is 21. The average number of participants in each auction is 19, and only 13 to 15 primary dealers are allocated on average. The average number of bids is quite large, about 60 in the main sample, representing an average of three bids per bidder. Of these, only 26 bids on average are allocated. The bid-to-cover ratio,COVER⁠, is the ratio between the total amount bid and the allocated amount. The average bid-to-cover ratio is about 2, reaching an average of 2.52 in 2018, another sign of the liquidity of the market.
article.4 Data description and elasticity measur:
Our main data, described above, is a unique bid-level data obtained from the IGCP. From Bloomberg, we obtain the secondary-market bid, ask, and mid prices at the daily frequency for all T-Bonds for the same time span. As a benchmark for market performance, we collect also from Bloomberg the total return index of Portuguese government bonds.9We also obtain from Bloomberg the spread between 10-year Portuguese T-bonds and German Bunds of the same maturity.

4.1 Price elasticity of demand

IfPandQdescribe price and quantity demanded at the priceP, respectively, then the price elasticity of demand is(∂Q/∂P)(P/Q)⁠. A large (absolute) value of the elasticity means that small price decreases are associated with large increases in demanded quantities. Thus, with high elasticity, a shock to supply, such as a pre-announced re-opening through an auction, is absorbed by demand without much of a price decrease.

The main question when estimating the price elasticity is which point in the demand curve to use. The measure of the price elasticity of demand that is the focus of this paper uses bids from untapped liquidity. It measures the marginal decrease in the price needed for the IGCP to increase the quantity sold beyond the auctioned amount. This measure is close in spirit to the second measure calculated byKandel, Sarig, and Wohl (1999), who use all unfilled orders. To calculate this elasticity, we take the four price points from unsubscribed bids next to the cutoff price, together with the cutoff price point itself. These five price points generally correspond to more than five bids, as more than one dealer may submit a bid at the same price point: across all auctions, the 25th (75th) percentile of the number of bids used in the calculation of the elasticity is seven (nine). The five pairs (Qi,Pi) are constructed such thatQiequals the sum of the quantities bid at price pointPior higher. In 38% of the auctions, all of the quantity bid at the cutoff price is allocated. In the rest of the auctions, a small quantity is not allocated. This unsubscribed quantity represents on average about 2.4% of the total allocated amount or 25% of the total quantity bid at the cutoff price. In practice, this means that, in these auctions, the IGCP could have increased quantity marginally at the same price. Even though this represents a tiny portion of the demand curve, it is a portion with infinite elasticity of demand, nonetheless. To take this into account, in every auction with a pro rata allocation, we add to the quantity-price point at the cutoff, (Qc,Pc), one additional point, (Qa,Pc), such thatQais the allocated quantity at the cutoff. The robustness section contains additional analysis regarding rationing.

Using these quantity-price pairs, we estimate a linear regression model ofQionPiand a constant. The slope in the model is an estimate of∂Q/∂P⁠. We multiply this estimated slope by the ratio of the cutoff price to the cutoff quantity to get an elasticity. All elasticity estimates are negative. We take the natural logarithm of the negative of the estimated elasticity and call this measure themarginal elasticity of demand,ME⁠.

SinceMEmeasures untapped liquidity, we ask whether the untapped liquidity comes from allocated bidders or from unallocated bidders (or new participants). We find that, on average, across all auctions, 73% of the bidded value in the points used to calculateMEis attributed to allocated dealers. Conversely, on average only 27% of the bidded value in those points is new (or unallocated) participants. In terms of the number of dealers in the unallocated points used to constructME⁠, we find that on average only 30% of the bidders in those points have not been allocated. We conclude that a substantial portion of the bid value and of the bidders in the untapped liquidity used to calculateMEis from dealers who are already being allocated at the cutoff price. This is to be expected, as bidders strive to be allocated. As mentioned above, the Treasury evaluates dealers on a scorecard that depends, among other things, on dealers’ allocation in the competitive phase of the auctions. Performance in this scorecard is used to select dealers as lead managers of syndicated deals, a much coveted designation.

Different points of the demand curve may reveal different information. It is useful to construct other measures of the elasticity of demand as a way to benchmark the variableME⁠. Total elasticity,TE⁠, uses all the demand price points to estimate the slope∂Q/∂Pfrom a linear regression ofQonPand a constant. Gross elasticity,GE⁠, is obtained from the slope of the demand curve estimated using only the maximum price and the cutoff price points and corresponding bidded quantities. Subscribed elasticity,SE⁠, uses the four price points (possibly the same number of bids or more) fromsubscribedbids next to the cutoff price, together with the cutoff price point.SEis similar toKandel, Sarig, and Wohl (1999)’s first measure, which uses the last filled orders in the auction. These measures differ fromMEonly in the estimated slope since the other term in these elasticities,P/Q, is identical.Figure 1plots the aggregate demand curve for a 10-year T-bond auctioned on May 11, 2016. Depicted in the figure are the slopes of the lines used to estimateME,TE,GE⁠, andSE⁠. For the auction depicted in the figure, the values ofME,TE,GE⁠, andSE(all expressed in logarithms) are 5.23, 4.89, 5.60, and 6.21 respectively. The demand curve depicted in the figure contains a quasi-kink close to the cutoff price of the auction, a feature common to many auctions in our sample, which is responsible for the lower values ofMEandTErelative toGEandSE⁠. We return to the significance of this demand kink in subsection 4.4.

Fig. 1 Example of an auction demand curve The figure presents all the bid prices for the auction of May 11, 2016 of a 10-year Treasury Bond. The range of bids was between 96.00 and 97.13 with a cut-off price of 96.78. The secondary market price at the end of the day on the same bond was 96.993. The IGCP indicated it would like to issue between €750 million and €1 billion, the bid amount was €1.83 billion, and the final allocated amount was €1.15 billion. The figure also presents four slopes used to construct four different elasticity measures. Marginal represents the slope of the demand curve using untapped demand, given by the cut-off price point and 4 unsubscribed price points to the right of the cut-off price. Total represents the slope of the demand curve using all price points. Gross represents the slope of the demand curve using just the cut-off price point and the maximum price point. Subscribed represents the slope of the demand curve using the last of the subscribed bids, given by the cut-off price point and 4 subscribed price points to the left of the cut-off price. These slopes yield the elasticity values for M E of 5.23, T E of 4.89, G E of 5.60, and S E of 6.21, respectively. Open in new tab Download slide

AsFigure 1illustrates,MEis likely to contain different information from that inSE,GEorTE⁠.GEandTEmay be distorted by extreme bids, namely bids of relatively small quantities at very high prices. These bids guarantee that the dealer receives an allocation, since the cutoff price is likely to be significantly lower. Since dealer allocation (even of a small quantity) helps determine of side benefits identified above, this bidding behavior may be strategic and not revealing of dealers’ valuations of the specific bond being auctioned. To alleviate this concern, the IGCP introduced in 2017 an overbidding penalty affecting dealers’ evaluation scorecard, which suggests that this type of behavior has indeed occurred (seeAFME/Finance for Europe 2020).

Table 2contains descriptive statistics of the various elasticity measures. All of the elasticity measures display relatively large figures. (Note that these numbers are expressed in logarithms, whereas the number reported in the introduction of an average estimated marginal elasticity of 332 is calculated without applying logarithms.) ThatMEis smaller on average thanSE⁠, as in the example above, likely reflects the quasi-kink present in many of the demand curves in the sample.

4.2 Auxiliary variables

Table 2contains descriptive statistics of other variables used in the analysis. (Table A.1 in the appendixcontains all the variable definitions.)SIZEandCOVERare defined and described above.RBASis the average of the previous five-trading day period (excluding the auction day) of the daily relative bid-ask spread of the same bond being auctioned, calculated as the difference between the ask and the bid prices divided by the mid price (in percent). The average relative bid-ask spread is 0.24%.DRIFTis the log return of the bond being auctioned computed from the end of day on Thursday to the end of day on Tuesday prior to the auction (in percent) adjusted for the return on Bloomberg’s index of Portuguese government bonds over the same period times a shrinkage beta. There is a negative drift of nine basis points on average, with a standard deviation that is about three times as large as the absolute value of its mean.SPREADis the average of the previous five-trading day period (excluding the auction day) of the difference between the 10-year Portuguese T-bond and the 10-year Bund (in basis points). The average spread in the sample is about 216 basis points. The spread declined significantly over the sample as the Portuguese economy improved, so the higher values are generally from the earlier part of the sample, and the lower values from the later part of the sample.VOLis the standard deviation of bond’s daily log returns in the secondary market over the 20 trading days prior to the auction date. The average daily volatility of log returns is 0.42%.SDURis a dummy equal to one for bonds of duration shorter than the median duration of the bonds in the sample (ie, 8.3 years). We use the Macaulay measure, and, as an alternative, we redo the tests using the bonds’ residual maturity. The correlation between the two duration dummies is 0.98, and usurprisingly our results are unaffected when the dummy with bond maturity is used instead.

We also consider the effect of Eurosystem’s purchases of Portuguese government bonds in the secondary market that occurred from March 2015 onwards. Detailed data on Eurosystem purchases are proprietary and available only to their researchers. However, the extent of Eurosystem’s presence in the secondary market was generally well anticipated by market participants given the implementation rules of the purchase programs and the regularity of the trading that followed.10ECBpreis the normalized value of purchases conducted by both the ECB and the Bank of Portugal in the 10 days prior to the blackout period of the auction.Table 2reports that purchases prior to an auction were on average 12% of the expected issue size (calculated only for auctions after March 2015).

Table 3presents the linear correlations between variables. Larger auctions (⁠SIZE⁠) are associated with lower bid-ask spreads (⁠RBAS⁠), suggesting that the IGCP sees a market appetite for larger auctions in lower pre-auction bid-ask spreads. HigherSPREADand higherVOLare associated with higherRBASand with lowerDRIFT⁠.SDURcorrelates positively withSPREAD⁠, suggesting that the IGCP tends to issue shorter duration bonds when the spread on the 10-year Portuguese bond to the 10-year German Bund rises.ECBprecorrelates negatively withSDUR⁠, suggesting that the ECB is more active prior to auctions of longer-maturity bonds.

4.3 Determinants of the price elasticity of demand

The price elasticity describes the ability of demand to absorb a supply shock, and as such it measures liquidity. InKyle (1989), the slope of the demand curve carries two components, one linked to risk aversion and the risk-bearing capacity of informed investors and another linked to market liquidity associated with the presence or lack thereof of noise traders.Table 3shows that, unsurprisingly, all elasticity measures are strongly positively correlated with each other. The elasticity measures correlate negatively with the relative bid-ask spread, especiallyGEandTE⁠. (Only the correlation betweenSEandRBASis statistically insignificant.) All the elasticity measures also correlate significantly withVOL⁠, displaying correlations between –0.68 and –0.29. These high correlations suggest that there is common information between the elasticity measures and bothRBASandVOL⁠. Specifically, they may all share complementary information about liquidity (see alsoAllen, Hortaçsu, and Kastl 2021). The elasticity measures correlate positively withCOVER⁠, an indication that deals that are highly demanded relative to the allocated amount are also deals where the demand elasticity and hence the risk-bearing capacity is highest. Somewhat unexpectedly, the elasticity does not display a statistically significant correlation with auction size.

We study the cross-sectional determinants of the marginal elasticity by running a multivariate regression ofMEonRBAS,DRIFT,SIZE,SPREAD,VOL,SDUR⁠, andECBpre⁠. The results are displayed inTable 4. We also include a dummyD_ECBthat equals one prior to March 2015, when the ECB’s Asset Purchase Programme (APP) started. The dummy variable is used to distinguish a zero in the continuous variable, due to no purchases of the auctioned bond versus due to the absence of the asset purchase program. Only the first two sets of regressions includeVOL⁠.11The regression specifications allow for year and quarter fixed effects (second and fourth columns).

WhenVOLis present, it is the only statistically significant variable together withECBpre⁠. One standard deviation increase inVOLis associated with a decrease inMEof about 0.09, equivalent to about 13% of a standard deviation ofME⁠. The strong correlation betweenVOLandMEsuggests that they capture similar aspects of liquidity, arising possibly from inventory risk, the effect thatVOLhas on risk-bearing capacity through dealers’ portfolio constraints (see alsoGoldstein and Hotchkiss 2020for the U.S. corporate bond market), or from time variation in risk premia.RBAS⁠, which has a statistically significant negative correlation withME⁠, is insignificantly related after controlling for the other variables.DRIFTshows an insignificant negative association withMEin the regressions whereVOLis included. The lack of significance could be explained by the existence of residual uncertainty about the size of the auction that is only resolved at the auction (Sigaux 2020). Shorter duration bonds have higherME⁠, but the effect is insignificant in the regressions whereVOLis included.

Table 4also shows that higher ECB purchases prior to an auction are associated with a higherMEwhen no fixed effects are included. The effect is statistically insignificant, once time fixed effects are added to the regression, an indication that perhaps some quarter-year observations saw systematically more intense purchasing by the ECB. The positive association between ECB pre-auction purchases andMEis consistent with our hypothesis that the higher elasticity stems from the increase in risk bearing capacity for two reasons. First, when primary dealers sell the bond to the ECB in the pre-auction period, they reduce their inventories of the auctioned bond prior to the auction. Second, primary dealers can forecast high demand by the ECB in the post-auction period if they observe (even if with some noise) high pre-auction ECB purchases. (In untabulated results, the correlation between ECB pre-auction purchases and post-auction purchases is 0.69 and is statistically significant at the 1% level or better.)12Also consistent with the risk-bearing hypothesis is the observation that there is no correlation betweenMEand ECB purchases when the later is normalized by the bond’s outstanding amount; it is the supply shock from the auction that needs to be absorbed, independent of the outstanding amount of the bond (untabulated).

4.4 IGCP behavior and the cutoff price

The negative difference observed on average betweenMEandSE(orGE⁠) suggests that the demand curve often depicts a quasi-kink around the cut-off price (seeTable 2andFigure 1). While the elasticity at any given point of the demand curve is completely determined by bidder behavior, the elasticity observed around the cutoff price also depends on the particular choice of cutoff price by the seller, because supply is not fixed but is chosen at the auction by the IGCP.

Keloharju, Nyborg, and Rydqvist (2005)discuss the strategic behavior of the Finnish Treasury when conducting uniform-price bond auctions and show that the seller usually chooses the cutoff price to maximize marginal revenue (marginal revenue defined with respect to quantity). Although this strategy does not maximize the total revenue in any particular auction, it may be justified by the fact that the Treasury repeatedly engages the market using auctions. They argue that, if the Treasury were to maximize revenue in any one auction, it would choose the minimum bid price at that auction, which would likely have a significant negative impact in the secondary market and compromise future auctions.Keloharju, Nyborg, and Rydqvist (2005)argue that, by maximizing the marginal revenue around the most preferred price among bidders, the Treasury promotes more competition in subsequent auctions.

We find that IGCP follows a similar strategy.Figure 3plots for intervals of €0.01 around the auction cutoff price—with the central bar representing the cutoff price—the proportion of auctions where the marginal revenue is maximized. Compared to prices in the vicinity of the cutoff price, there is a significantly higher fraction of auctions where the marginal revenue is maximized at exactly the cutoff price. Our sample also reveals that the IGCP typically chose an issuance amount equal to the top of the indicative range announced. In double auction days, which constitute the majority of our auction days, the IGCP can both issue at the upper bound of the indicative interval (the issuance interval refers to the sum of the two auction sizes) and for at least one of the auctions choose a cutoff price that maximizes marginal revenue. Conditioning on double auctions, the fraction of auctions where the marginal revenue is maximized at exactly the cutoff price is 37%, higher than observed inFigure 3for the full sample. Further evidence is presented in theonline appendix. There we present a plot of the average bid amount for intervals of €0.01 around the auction cutoff price and show that demand at the cutoff price is generally significantly higher, with about twice the bid amount relative to any other price in the vicinity.13

Choosing the cutoff point to maximize marginal revenue implies that the IGCP picks a point on the demand curve where the elasticity of demand estimated using price points to the left of the cutoff price (⁠SE⁠) is significantly higher than that estimated using price points to the right of the cutoff price (⁠ME⁠), possibly a demand kink.14BecauseMEuses unsubscribed bids, we hypothesize that it captures the primary dealers’ remaining risk-bearing capacity in the aftermath of a bond auction (ie, untapped liquidity). In the next section, we relateMEwith the post-auction abnormal return. IncludingSEin the regressions allows us to control for the tapped liquidity in the market and any kink in demand. We also control forGEinstead ofSEin a robustness test available in theonline appendix.
article.5 Secondary-market price dynamics around:
In this section, we analyze the secondary market price of the bond being auctioned around the auction day. Our hypothesis is that the marginal elasticity captures dealers’ expectations of their ability to sell in the secondary market the recently acquired bonds. When capital is slow to respond in the short term, dealers’ may expect that they have to hold more of the issued bonds for longer. These expectations reduce their risk-bearing capacity and are reflected in a lower marginal elasticity of demand. We therefore expect that a low elasticity of demand is associated with a slow price adjustment in the secondary market. AsDuffie (2010)argues, the response to a supply shock “typically involves […] a subsequent and more extended reversal” (see also the discussions inKaul, Mehrotra, and Morck 2000, andHendershott et al. 2022). We first analyze secondary-market bond prices in a window of 11 trading days centered at the auction day. Second, we conduct in-sample predictive return regressions onMEand other variables. Third, we conduct a cross-sectional test focusing on bond duration. Overall, our tests complement the literature by showing that demand conditions matter for how the shock is absorbed.

5.1 Event-study analysis

We plot the cumulative log abnormal return (ie, the adjusted price) in the secondary market of the bond being auctioned starting five trading days prior to the auction date and ending five trading days afterward. Because Portuguese government bond prices showed a significant upward trend through most of the sample period, using prices allows us to subtract the log return of Bloomberg’s index of Portuguese government bonds, which we multiply by a shrinkage beta (seeVasicek 1973andFrazzini and Pedersen 2014), from the log return of the bond being auctioned to control for these market trends. In theonline appendix, we show that the results are qualitatively the same using the log-daily bond return in excess of the log-daily bond market index return.Lou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)conduct their analysis using yields instead of prices, and, in the robustness section, we report on results from an analysis that uses raw yields.

The top panel ofFigure 4displays the average cumulative log abnormal return (solid line) and corresponding 90% confidence bands (grey area). Note that day 0 represents the closing price at the end of the auction day, which we normalize to 0. The top panel of the figure shows that there is a price decline from end-of-day -4 to end-of-day -1, with a large drop occurring on day -3, the Friday before the auction when the line(s) to be auctioned is (are) announced. This price decline starts to reverse on the day of the auction (recall that the day 0 price is the closing price on the day of the auction) and generally continues to increase in the days following it. The mean price decline prior to the auction is the mean ofDRIFT(of nine bps). Skipping the day of the auction, the abnormal log return in the five days following the auction is about six basis points. Price reversal is not immediate, similar to other instances of price pressure.15This V-shaped pattern is consistent with the evidence ofLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)for U.S. Treasury auctions of an inverted V-shaped pattern in yields around auction dates.

In the bottom panel ofFigure 4, we split the auction sample by the median value ofME⁠. Under the hypothesis that the V-shaped pattern in prices is due to primary dealers’ limited risk-bearing capacity, we expect to find this pattern primarily when the elasticity of demand is low. For periods of high elasticity (black line), there is no significant price change through the event window. However, for periods of low elasticity (gray line), the V-shaped price pattern is more pronounced than the unconditional pattern in the top panel of the figure. In the subsample of low elasticity, the average price drift prior to the auction is close to -11 bps, and the log abnormal return in the five days after the auction is about 21 basis points. This suggests that the liquidity left untapped by the IGCP, as captured byME⁠, contains information about the remaining risk-bearing capacity of primary dealers. In the robustness section, we conduct the same analysis usingSE,TE⁠, andGE⁠. Previewing our results, we find the same V-shaped pattern in prices but weaker significance, suggesting thatMEbetter proxies for dealers’remainingrisk-bearing capacity. Two reasons discussed above may account forMEbeing a less noisy proxy of dealers’ risk-bearing capacity: the choice of cutoff price and dealers’ overbidding.

5.2 Predictive regressions

We run a series of cross-sectional regressions of the log abnormal return for auction i measured from the close on the auction day to trading day h after the auction, A R i , h ⁠ . For each h , we estimate the model β ϵ A R i , h = β X i + ϵ i , h , (1) where the variables X i include the marginal elasticity, ME i , and control variables for auction i . We normalize the variables X i by their respective sample standard deviation for ease of interpretation of economic significance. The control variables are the relative bid-ask spread ( ⁠ RBAS ⁠ ), the price drift prior to the auction ( ⁠ DRIFT ⁠ ), the auction size ( ⁠ SIZE ⁠ ), the bid-to-cover ratio ( ⁠ COVER ⁠ ), the spread between Portuguese and German 10-year T-Bonds ( ⁠ SPREAD ⁠ ), the return volatility of the bond being auctioned ( ⁠ VOL ⁠ ), and the elasticity from subscribed bids ( ⁠ S E ⁠ ). A liquidity premium would suggest a positive coefficient associated with RBAS ⁠ . DRIFT and SIZE are both expected to load negatively, given the evidence of Lou, Yan, and Zhang (2013) . VOL is expected to be positively associated with the holding period return because it relates to both market liquidity and time-varying risk premia. And higher COVER should imply greater liquidity and be associated with a lower return or, as Beetsma et al. (2018) , predict with high returns, as it proxies for investors’ valuations.

Our regression model differs from the regression models ofBeetsma et al. (2016)andBeetsma et al. (2018)in two ways. Their models use the time series of the daily yield or daily yield change over the whole sample (the dependent variables of interest). They regress these variables on a dummy for days when auctions occur (or days with nearby auctions) possibly interacted with other variables. Using time series data brings in a problem of overlapping observations when the holding period horizon is longer than one day, as in our exercise. We avoid this concern by running cross-sectional regressions with non-overlapping observations. In addition, our regression model is predictive in the sense that our dependent variable is measured after the auction. We do this to test whether the price change that follows the auction is positively associated with an existing risk premium, as captured byME⁠.16Ideally, we would measure returns starting shortly after the auction results are announced, but, because we only have daily data for the benchmark of Portuguese bonds from Bloomberg, we measure returns from the close of the auction day. We expect to still be able to capture the effects from price pressure, as these tend to take time to reverse (e.g.,Duffie 2010). Our regression model is closer in spirit to the work ofLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)because they also use cross-sectional, predictive, in sample regressions. However, they measure returns from a trading strategy that spans a window that is centered in the auction day and thus cannot use information that is available only at the auction day such asMEorCOVER⁠.

Table 5summarizes the results for a specific horizon of five days (h= 5) after the auction date,AR5, to match the evidence presented inFigure 4. The first two columns show regressions withoutMEbut where we include variables previously proposed in the literature. They serve as a benchmark for our results. The other three columns includeMEas an independent variable. The results show thatRBASis significantly positively associated with the five-day return, consistent withRBAScapturing a liquidity premium associated with transaction costs (e.g.,Albuquerque, Song, and Yao 2020). As the regressors have been standardized by dividing them by the respective sample standard deviation, we infer that one standard deviation increase inRBASis associated with a 10 to 13 basis point increase in the five-day post-auction return. The bid-to-cover ratio does not have any predictive ability. This result contrasts with the evidence ofBeetsma et al. (2018)and may be due to the return-measurement timing assumptions explained above.SIZEis negatively associated with future returns, but the coefficients are statistically insignificant. Other control variables do not have a statistically significant predictive power, except forSE⁠, which measures the marginal elasticity of demand allocated in the auction and is used to control for the kink in demand around the cutoff price.

Table 5shows thatMEis a significant predictor of returns.MEis statistically significant at the 1% level or better and carries a negative coefficient estimate in all specifications, as predicted. In addition, addingMEto the regression contributes to an increase in the adjustedR2between seven and eight percentage points, depending on the specification. Since the table displays standardized coefficients, an estimate of –11.37 in the last column implies that a one standard deviation decrease inMEtranslates into an 11 basis point price increase following the auction. This evidence is consistent withMEproxying for dealers’ risk-bearing capacity.

Next,Table 6presents results that use the specification in the last column ofTable 5but with returns measured at different horizons (⁠h=1,2,…,10⁠). The table shows that the coefficient associated withMEis negative at all horizons and is statistically significant, except at horizon of two days.RBASis positive and significant at most horizons.DRIFTis always negative and significant at the shorter horizons, indicating that some of the pre-auction price movement reverts, independent ofME⁠. The other controls are mostly statistically insignificant, except forSE⁠. To study further the role of the demand kink, we run a regression where we include onlyME—SE. Overall,ME—SEdoes the same job asMEandSEseparately, which is expected, since the estimated coefficients in the unconstrained version of the model are of about the same size but have opposite signs for these two variables.

In theonline appendix, we present results where we excludeVOLfrom the regression becauseVOLandMEare strongly correlated (seeTable 4) and they may proxy for the same effect. Adding all controls butVOLresults in higher estimated coefficients associated withMEwith somewhat higher significance. We conclude that the evidence is consistent withMEbeing a proxy for primary dealers’ ability to absorb the supply shock and a better proxy thanVOL⁠.

Theonline appendixpresents results where we study the effects fromECBpre⁠. IncludingECBpreas a control in the predictive regressions does not significantly improve the models’ ability to explain future returns, nor does it affectME’s ability to explain future returns. Further, allowing for the sensitivity of returns toMEto vary with ECB purchases does not qualitatively change our main results. The results suggest that the information content of the ECB demand is relevant during the auction cycle (as it is correlated withME⁠) but, with regard to the post-auction price variation, it is subsumed byME⁠. This evidence comports with that reported byvan Spronsen and Beestma (2022)that ECB purchases tend to dampen auction cycles (ie, the observed V-shape pattern in prices). We find that the dampening appears to occur indirectly through the effect of ECB purchases onME⁠.

We re-estimate the predictive regressions evaluating the separate contribution of other controls: the sum across all bidders of the squared shares of bidded quantities (this variable captures concentration of allocations and the possibility that some investors corner the market); the average daily order flow in the 10 days prior to the auction (this variable captures bond liquidity prior to the auction as costs of immediacy as inChacko, Jurek, and Stafford 2008); the difference between the observed yield in the day prior to the announcement day and the corresponding fit of a yield curve using theSvensson (1994)model in the announcement day as estimated by the ECB (this variable proxies for the strength of demand of the bond being auctioned); the fraction of filled orders at the cutoff price to capture any remaining effect from a pro rata allocation; the ratio of the expected size of the auction (either the maximum of the indicative interval, or, when two bonds are auctioned, the maximum of the indicative interval divided across the two bonds using the proportion of each bond actually issued) to the outstanding amount of the bond before the auction date; the deviation of the auction size from the upper limit of the indicative interval (seeSigaux 2020); a dummy variable that takes the value one if the bond being auctioned had a syndication earlier in the same year (typically these are new on-the-run bonds on a benchmark maturity in strong demand); and a dummy variable that takes the value one when the current bond was auctioned in at least one of the previous three auction dates. The results, displayed in theonline appendix, are qualitatively unchanged.

5.3 Bond duration: A cross-sectional test

This subsection provides a cross-sectional test that the elasticity of demand relates to dealers’ risk bearing capacity. Longer-duration bonds weigh negatively on dealers’ risk bearing capacity for four reasons. First, they carry higher interest rate risk and are thus more prone to impact traders’ overall profits. Second, while all public debt securities are eligible to serve as collateral in the ECB’s open-market operations, they are subject to different valuation haircuts that increase with the residual bond maturity. Third, and perhaps more important given our conversations with IGCP, dealers’ appear to have less willingness to trade longer-duration bonds, which the IGCP counters by weighing dealers’ performance in the competitive auction phase by duration of the bond being auctioned. Fourth, inTable 3, shorter-duration bonds tend to have more elastic demand (ie,SDURcorrelates positively withME⁠, as well as with other elasticity measures), which suggests that dealers have a higher risk-bearing capacity for shorter-duration bonds on average. We expect a differential effect ofMEon post-auction bond prices for longer-duration bonds.

We repeat the predictive regressions includingSDURand an interaction term betweenMEandSDUR⁠. The results are available inTable 7. The economic and statistical significance ofMEis higher in these regressions compared to those inTable 6, and the coefficient associated withMEis now statistically significant across all holding period returns. As the estimated coefficient of the interaction term betweenMEandSDURis negative but lower in absolute value than the estimated coefficient onME⁠, risk-bearing capacity concerns still appear relevant for shorter-duration bonds, but the effect is statistically insignificant (untabulated t-statistics). This evidence is consistent withMEcapturing risk-bearing capacity concerns that appear particularly relevant for longer-duration bonds.17Using the level of bond duration as opposed to a dummy variable leads to similar results, but these results are not as easily interpretable.

article.6 Robustness analysis:
Dealers’ strategic bidding

Thus far, we have assumed that the elasticity of demand proxies for dealers’ risk-bearing capacity. However, because the elasticity of demand is calculated with bids, dealers’ strategic bidding can also influence the elasticity. One concern is that of bid shading, a practice documented in many countries and across different auction formats, of bidding below ones’ valuation (e.g.,Nyborg, Rydqvist, and Sundaresan 2002andHortaçsu, Kastl, and Zhang 2018). It can lead to underpricing relative to the secondary market. In Portugal, bid shading may be mitigated by benefits that the IGCP gives to dealers—syndication fees and post-auction noncompetitive offerings—that depend on their allocations across multiple auctions (Cardoso-Costa et al. 2022). In fact, Treasury bond auctions in Portugal were on average overpriced in our sample. In addition, Treasury bond auctions in Portugal often are rationed (see below), which can be optimal to minimize bid shading (Parlour and Rajan 2005).

To study the effect of bid shading in our results, we construct two variables that proxy for bid shading: underpricing (UP), measured as the difference between the secondary-market price and the cutoff auction price; bid discount (DISC), measured as the cross-dealer average of the difference between the secondary-market price and the quantity weighted average bid price of each dealer. To estimateUPandDISCwith precision, we use MTS transaction-level data. As indicated in footnote 9, quoted prices in their database have firm commitment, and we treat them as good as transaction prices. We have the three best quotes on each side of the market, which are almost always available from 9 am to 5:30 pm CET. We computeDISCandUPusing the average mid-price (average between best bid and best ask) in the 15 minutes from 9:45 am and 10:00 am, immediately before the auction.18Theonline appendixshows results from a replication ofTables 4and6including these new controls. First, we find that lowerDISCandUP, meaning higher bid prices relative to secondary-market prices, are associated with higher elasticity. Note that, likeCOVER⁠, these two variables are observable only at the time of the auction and thus cannot be used to predictME⁠. Second, we find that including these new variables does not affect the ability ofMEto predict holding period returns. Also, the new controls are themselves insignificant in the predictive regressions, with the exception ofDISCfor horizons of three and four days after the auction. Overall, the results suggest that the demand schedule from the auction has important information content regarding dealers’ risk-bearing capacity, even when we control for variables that describe dealers’ strategic behavior.

Alternative measures of elasticity

We study the significance of usingMEversus any of the other estimated elasticities. The relevant question is where in the demand curve should we estimate an elasticity of demand that best captures dealers’ risk-bearing capacity? In theonline appendix, we repeat the event-study analysis where we split the sample using each of the other proposed elasticity measures. As withME⁠, forTE,GE⁠, andSE⁠, there is a V-shape pattern in abnormal returns around auctions only in the low elasticity subsamples, though the shape is less pronounced. In addition, as noted when discussing the predictive regressions, these measures do not show that same power to predict post-auction excess returns at some horizons. A possible reason is the noise associated with the problem of overbidding. We interpret our combined findings as revealing that dealers’ risk-bearing capacity is best identified by the marginal elasticity associated with untapped, residual liquidity.

As the elasticity captures both the slope and a scaling factor, we further analyze whether our results are driven by variation in the scaling factor or in the slope of the demand curve. An elasticity measures relative quantity/price changes in percentage points and thus has the advantage of being more directly comparable across auctions of different size and prices than the slope of the demand curve. However, dealers sell the bonds in the secondary market, where there are substitutes to the bond being auctioned (in fact, the same bond in the Portuguese case), and the relevant scaling factor may be the outstanding amount of the bond being issued. As alternatives, we defineMEusing, as the scaling quantities, the outstanding amount of the auctioned bond prior to and after the auction. We also consider the slope of the demand function without any scaling. Theonline appendixreports summary statistics of these elasticity/slope measures and correlations between them and other variables. All measures are strongly correlated with each other and show similar correlations with other variables. The average elasticity of demand scaled by the auction amount is 332 as reported above, but, when we scale by the outstanding amount of the corresponding bond, it drops to 33. Theonline appendixshows that our main results are robust to these alternative specifications, which we interpret as evidence that it is variation in the slope of the demand curve and not in the scaling factor that matters for our results.

Theonline appendixalso presents results using several variants ofME⁠: using only two or three price points from unsubscribed bids plus the cutoff price point and using all the price points of the unsubscribed bids next to the cutoff point that fall within a fixed price interval. Except when using only two price points from unsubscribed bids to calculateME⁠, which generates a much more volatile measure of the elasticity of demand, the results are qualitatively the same.

Placebo analysis

We conduct a placebo test by examining unused auction dates by the IGCP. Recall that the IGCP can issue on the second, fourth, and fifth Wednesdays of each month but leaves many of these dates unused. There are 82 unused auction dates between April 2014 and the end of 2019.19In theonline appendix, we replicate the graphical event-study analysis of the top plot inFigure 4for bonds of three maturities: two, five, and 10 years. Specifically, at each unused auction date, the secondary-market prices of these bonds do not present the V-shaped pattern observed around executed auctions. Interestingly, the abnormal return of the 10-year bond seems to increase throughout the window, while the abnormal returns of the two-year and five-year bonds decrease. The trends observed over the 11-day windows suggest under- or over-performance relative to the aggregate index throughout the sample period. The 10-year bond’s upward trend may be justified by the fact that this is usually the most liquid on-the-run bond in the sample. The sample coincides with a period of search for yield in international bond markets, especially in long-term bonds. This was also supported by the ECB’s Asset Purchase Programme, which is consistent with the relative under-performance observed for two- and five-year bonds.

Yields versus prices

We run the event-study analysis using yields instead of prices, to confirm the statistical and economic significance of the impact of the marginal elasticity on price pressure on the secondary market. Yields encompass in a sole measure the heterogeneity of features between different Treasury bonds, namely maturity and coupon rate. However, the advantage of using prices—and the reason that we chose prices for the main analysis—is the fact that we can compute abnormal returns by adjusting Treasury bond raw returns using the Portuguese Treasury bond index returns.

In theonline appendix, we plot the evolution of the raw yields of the bonds being auctioned. As expected, consistent with the results above, yields around auction dates follow an inverted V-shape, with an average reduction of three bps in the five-day post-auction period. The order of magnitude of these movements comports with the results obtained byLou, Yan, and Zhang (2013)orBeetsma et al. (2016). In addition, the inverted V-shape is only present in auctions where the marginal elasticity of demand is low.

Further, we run predictive regressions using yield cumulative changes from the auction day toh-days ahead rather than log-abnormal returns. Theonline appendixshows that the coefficient estimates associated withMEon these regressions are positive and significant, which is expected since there is a negative relationship between prices and yields. Thus, the results are robust to the use of yield changes.

The COVID-19 pandemic

The COVID-19 pandemic brought about significant volatility in financial markets that may have affected the risk-bearing capacity of primary dealer banks. On the one hand, this additional volatility should work as an ideal test to the risk-bearing capacity hypothesis. On the other, the unexpected nature, timing, and magnitude of the events mean that no risk model can fully account for what happened. For this last reason, we do not include the year of 2020 in the main analysis so not to taint results with an atypical year. However, the auctions in the COVID-19 pandemic era are interesting on their own.

The only auctions conducted in March 2020 occurred on the 11th. March 11 is also the day the World Health Organization declared COVID-19 a pandemic. The next day, the ECB announced arguably timid policy initiatives to contain the macroeconomic impact of the pandemic. At the press conference, ECB President Christine Lagarde said that the bank was “not here to close [bond] spreads. This is not the function or the mission of the ECB.”20In the ensuing days, government bond yields increased significantly in most euro area countries, including Portugal, until March 18, when the ECB announced the Pandemic Emergency Purchase Programme for €750 billion intended at ensuring a proper functioning of the monetary policy transmission mechanism by reducing the dispersion in spreads. The prices of the lines auctioned on the 11th (five-year and 10-year re-openings) fell dramatically in the days after the auction, conditioned by events that were arguably unanticipated at the time of the auction. Of note, the averageMEfor the two lines was low, 5.14 but only about half of one standard deviation away from the pre-pandemicMEaverage. Clearly, primary dealers did not price in the small demand presence after March 11, as the ECB shock could not have been anticipated. For this reason, in the analysis that follows, we exclude the two auctions from March 2020.

We repeat the predictive regressions including all the auctions in 2020, except for the two lines auctioned on March 11. The sample size increases to 82 observations. We report the predictive regressions results in theonline appendix. There are no qualitative changes relative to our main results. Focusing on the five-day horizon return results, the estimated coefficients associated withMEdrop slightly. The adjusted R-squares of all the regressions are quite similar, even those that excludeME⁠. While we would expect R-squares to increase due to an increase in the number of observations, the increased volatility in financial markets in the COVID-19 crisis, which makes the regressions including 2020 noisier, is likely to produce an offsetting effect. Overall, our findings are consistent with those displayed in the main analysis.
article.7 Conclusion:
We show that the elasticity of demand obtained from the primary market proxies for primary dealers’ risk-bearing capacity and thus should be studied as a potential source of information on risks about the aggregate economy. We revisit a common assumption in prominent financial-asset market models that supply shocks are absorbed with no price variation; ie, the price elasticity of demand is infinite. While the empirical finance literature has uncovered many examples where prices appear to move in response to supply shocks, even in the most liquid of markets, a possible manifestation of limited risk-bearing capacity in the short run, one step missing in this literature is to demonstrate that the observed price response is linked to the elasticity of demand. This paper uses observed aggregate demand data in auctions of sovereign debt to calculate the price elasticity of demand. It then shows that apparent price pressure in the secondary market around auction days is connected to the price elasticity of demand, suggesting that this elasticity captures dealers’ perceptions of the ease with which they can turn over the bonds purchased in the auction to their secondary-market clients.

From a policy perspective, policymakers’ monitoring of financial markets can benefit from using primary dealers’ price elasticity as a barometer to assess their risk-bearing capacity, as this is a higher frequency proxy for this capacity than others used in the literature (Goldberg 2020). In addition, issuers may benefit from knowing the value of the price elasticity of demand when determining the cutoff price of an auction, since the elasticity correlates with the price in secondary market in the days after the auction. (SeeAllen, Kastl, and Wittwer 2022for another argument on how knowing the elasticity of demand can be used to increase auction revenue.) Finally, understanding the price volatility induced by auctions, when and why it happens, can help banks develop better models of Value-at-Risk that use historical data to predict volatility. Banks use these models to determine their risk-bearing capacity, and auction-induced price volatility may be exactly the kind of volatility that banks should give more weight to.

In future work, and because changes in the price-elasticity of demand can signal changes in aggregate risk, we would like to study the predictive power of the price elasticity of demand over aggregate real and financial variables. Also, we would like to better understand the incentives of primary dealers in the selection of securities to be auctioned. Are primary dealers interested in securities whose demand is expected to be high post auction or securities whose price has been rising prior to the auction? In addition, there may be fewer auctions when demand is low, a dimension of liquidity in the extensive margin that remains understudied.

Multiplying both sides by P / Q and dividing both sides by E (taken to be non-zero), we get 1 + E + P 1 E ∂ E ∂ P = 0.
